en: In the writing, what was their relationship to the divine?
ko: 기록에서 그들의 신과의 관계는 어땠나요?

en: Relationship with the divine, well, the first thing to say is that they had a large pantheon of gods.
ko: 신과의 관계라면, 먼저 말씀드릴 건 그들에게 큰 판테온이 있었다는 겁니다.

en: So there were three gods at the top, sometimes called Anu, Enlil, and Ea.
ko: 꼭대기에 세 신이 있었는데, 아누, 엔릴, 에아라고 불리기도 했어요.

en: There were three gods at the top, and hundreds of other gods and goddesses.
ko: 꼭대기에 세 신이 있었고, 수백 명의 다른 신들이 있었어요.

en: And you have the situation that I think lots of small villages and towns had their old ancient gods, and eventually they were all worked into a kind of theological system like a phone book, and the lesser, minor gods were amalgamated, and then they were given jobs in the households of the big gods, so there was a sort of structure.
ko: 많은 작은 마을과 도시에 오래된 고대 신들이 있었고, 결국 전화번호부 같은 신학 체계로 통합됐어요. 하위 신들은 합쳐지고 큰 신들의 가정에 역할을 받았죠.

en: So you have this in the background, a big sweeping theology like you have in the world And this was the main system, and the main gods were concerned with the ruler and the fate of the country, and another god was concerned with illness and the dead and what happens to the dead, and they had other specialities, and they all had their own temples.
ko: 이런 배경으로 세계의 큰 신학처럼 거대한 신학이 있었고, 주요 신들은 통치자와 나라의 운명을 관장했고, 다른 신은 질병과 죽은 자를 관장했고, 각각 전문 분야가 있었고 모두 자체 신전이 있었어요.

en: And when a baby came into the world, probably this was universally true, the baby was put under the tutelage of one or other of the gods.
ko: 아기가 태어나면 아마 보편적으로 그랬을 텐데, 아기를 신 중 하나의 보호 아래 두었어요.

en: Sometimes the royal family, they were the big shots, but sometimes not, or the ones that were in the family or something like that.
ko: 왕족이면 거물 신이었지만, 때로는 가문의 신이거나 하는 식이었어요.

en: So people had, grew up with the idea that among all of them, there were special ones for the family, and they had a special one who was supposed to look after them.
ko: 사람들은 모든 신 중에 가족을 위한 특별한 신이 있고, 자기를 돌봐주기로 된 특별한 신이 있다는 생각을 하며 자랐어요.

en: That's the sort of basic idea. But the trouble is, since gods are, as you say, human beings on a larger scale, they can be forgetful or uninterested or on holiday, and there are lots of ways that you have to prompt your, make little sacrifices and little bribes so that they do their job and keep an eye on you.
ko: 기본적으로 그런 개념이에요. 하지만 문제는 신들이 인간의 확대판이니까 잊어버리거나 관심 없거나 휴가 중일 수 있고, 작은 제물이나 뇌물로 일을 하게 해야 했어요.

en: So they had that kind of slightly practical view of gods, that they were a bit unpredictable, great when they were there but not always there sort of idea.
ko: 그래서 신에 대해 약간 실용적인 관점이 있었어요. 좀 예측 불가능하고, 있을 때는 좋지만 항상 있는 건 아니라는.

en: And I also believe this, that a lot of people in the world today who did not have the disadvantage of growing up in a stifling religion, but are just normal people, get a lot more interested when they're really ill or when they have a big disaster all of a sudden.
ko: 오늘날 세계에서도 억압적인 종교 없이 자란 평범한 사람들이 정말 아프거나 큰 재난이 닥치면 갑자기 더 관심을 갖게 되잖아요.

en: God or gods seem a lot more important than they do normally, so that few people walk about in a state of religious awe, and a good proportion of clergymen I've ever met don't do that either.
ko: 신이 평소보다 훨씬 더 중요하게 느껴지는 거죠. 종교적 경외감 속에서 다니는 사람은 거의 없고, 만나본 성직자들 중에서도 마찬가지예요.

en: It's a kind of conception that's not actually based on reality, that the individual's response to religious stimuli fluctuates and is varied, and is often a response to need.
ko: 개인의 종교적 자극에 대한 반응은 변동하고 다양하며 필요에 대한 반응인 경우가 많다는, 현실에 기반하지 않은 개념이에요.

en: It doesn't come from nothing. I mean, people don't suddenly feel, I've got to thank the Lord for the rainbow or something like that.
ko: 아무 이유 없이 오는 게 아니에요. 무지개에 감사해야겠다고 갑자기 느끼는 사람은 없어요.

en: I think this is probably true today. When you read the investigations they make of religion today, Christianity in this country is on the decline, because people who are supposed to be Christians say they aren't anymore, they're atheists.
ko: 이건 아마 오늘날에도 사실일 거예요. 오늘날 종교 조사를 보면 이 나라에서 기독교는 쇠퇴하고 있어요.

en: And the people who say, I go to church and I believe in everything, is a relatively small number of people saying, now, this is the situation, which is quite remarkable if you think about it.
ko: 교회에 가고 모든 걸 믿는다는 사람은 상대적으로 소수예요. 이런 상황이라는 게 꽤 놀랍죠.

en: And the Lord knows what the consequence will be for the human race, whether religion will balance out, whether it will die off, who knows.
ko: 인류에게 어떤 결과가 올지, 종교가 균형을 잡을지 사라질지는 신만 아시겠죠.

en: I think it's an ancient technology that has proven across millennia to give a set of tools to humans to contend, as you said, with suffering.
ko: 종교는 수천 년에 걸쳐 인간에게 고통과 대면할 도구를 주는 것으로 입증된 고대 기술이라고 봐요.

en: That's a part of life. So when those rare moments come when you have to deal with deep pain, loss, suffering, heartbreak, all those things, looking up to the sky and asking questions and trying to figure out the answers in your conversation with the divine.
ko: 그게 삶의 일부니까요. 드문 순간에 깊은 고통, 상실, 고통, 비탄과 마주해야 할 때, 하늘을 올려다보고 질문하고 신과의 대화에서 답을 찾으려 하는 거죠.

en: I think that's true. But I think in Mesopotamia it was different in terms of its potency and immediacy, because there's no skyscrapers in Iraq.
ko: 맞다고 봐요. 하지만 메소포타미아에서는 그 강렬함과 즉시성이 달랐어요. 이라크에는 고층 건물이 없으니까요.

en: If you live in southern Iraq and you sleep on the roof, there are no lights at night.
ko: 남부 이라크에 살면서 지붕에서 자면 밤에 불빛이 없어요.

en: You know, you're under the stars. You can see everything because of no smog and everything like that.
ko: 별 아래 있는 거예요. 스모그 같은 게 없어서 다 보여요.

en: And the idea that the gods are there watching, it's not like a big artifice like it is here.
ko: 신들이 거기서 지켜보고 있다는 생각이 여기처럼 인위적이지 않아요.

en: It just doesn't ring true here. You can't come to it and really believe in it.
ko: 여기서는 진심으로 믿기 어려워요.

en: Whereas these people didn't have to really believe in it because it was it. It's the obvious practical part of life.
ko: 반면 그 사람들은 정말로 믿을 필요가 없었어요. 그냥 그런 거였으니까. 삶의 당연한 실용적 부분.

en: They're right there. Yeah. It's like they didn't believe in ghosts.
ko: 바로 거기 있으니까. 네. 귀신을 믿지 않았어요.

en: They took them for granted. And they didn't believe in the gods. They took them for granted. This is a different mechanism, because nobody here in the world today takes those things for granted.
ko: 당연하게 여겼어요. 신도 믿지 않았어요. 당연하게 여겼어요. 다른 메커니즘이에요. 오늘날 세계에서는 아무도 그런 걸 당연하게 여기지 않으니까.

en: Just the opposite. But I think that's how it worked. So you didn't have people wrestling with the idea of whether the gods really exist or that whether they really care about me.
ko: 정반대죠. 하지만 당시에는 그랬다고 봐요. 신이 정말 존재하는지 고민하는 사람이 없었어요.

en: They gave them a nudge when it was necessary, and they might offer this, they might offer that. But it was the system.
ko: 필요할 때 찔러주고, 이걸 바치고 저걸 바치고. 하지만 그게 시스템이었어요.

en: It was the prevailing system. And I think it's an important difference.
ko: 지배적인 시스템이었어요. 중요한 차이라고 봅니다.

en: And also that thing about ghosts is that it's clear from the inscriptions, all of them, that I managed to find, that nobody ever asked themselves, do these things exist or not?
ko: 그리고 유령에 대해서도, 제가 찾은 비문들에서 명확한 건 아무도 이것들이 존재하는지 자문하지 않았다는 거예요.

en: Did I really see them or not? Or did I not? They didn't. They didn't. They just took it all for granted. What are ghosts?
ko: 정말 봤나 안 봤나 묻지 않았어요. 그냥 당연하게 여겼어요. 유령이 뭔가요?

en: Is it usually ancestors? Well, everybody who died in bed, naturally or peacefully, what we call their ghost, went down to the netherworld.
ko: 보통 조상인가요? 편안히 또는 평화롭게 죽은 사람은 그 혼이 지하세계로 내려갔어요.

en: And there they were. So they buried people jolly quick, for obvious reasons.
ko: 거기 있었어요. 그래서 당연한 이유로 아주 빨리 묻었어요.

en: And like they do in Islam and Judaism today, it's the same kind of idea.
ko: 이슬람과 유대교에서 오늘날 하는 것처럼, 같은 개념이에요.

en: And the spirit of the person went down through the gates of the netherworld and stayed there.
ko: 사람의 영혼이 지하세계의 문을 통해 내려가서 거기 머물렀어요.

en: So that's the basic situation. And people in their houses had actual burials under the courtyard.
ko: 기본적으로 그런 상황이에요. 집 안뜰 아래에 실제 매장이 있었어요.

en: And they had a thing where you pour stuff down, a whole fluid and food, kind of symbolic offerings to them.
ko: 액체와 음식을 아래로 부어주는 장치가 있었는데, 상징적 제물 같은 거예요.

en: So is that a way to lessen the impact of mortality? I don't know.
ko: 그게 죽음의 충격을 줄이는 방법인가요? 모르겠어요.

en: Because you know everyone's going to die. I think the real tragedy would be is if we're not supposed to.
ko: 모든 사람이 죽을 테니까요. 정말 비극은 죽으면 안 되는 건데 죽는 거겠죠.

en: That would be the tragedy. But every single person is going to die.
ko: 그게 비극이겠죠. 하지만 모든 사람은 죽을 겁니다.

en: So all relationships have this finite clause in them.
ko: 모든 관계에는 유한한 조항이 있어요.

en: So if you're very fond of somebody or you love somebody and they die, it's kind of infantile to whine about it ever after.
ko: 누군가를 아끼거나 사랑하는데 그 사람이 죽으면, 그 후로 계속 징징대는 건 좀 유치해요.

en: Because what do you think was going to happen? Either you or them. You know, I always see it like that.
ko: 뭘 기대했어요? 당신 아니면 그 사람이죠. 저는 항상 그렇게 봐요.

en: I don't feel grief when people die. It is infantile. But I got to tell you something about human beings.
ko: 사람이 죽을 때 슬픔을 느끼지 않아요. 유치한 거예요. 하지만 인간에 대해 말씀드릴 게 있는데.

en: We're all kind of infantile all the way through from, you know, we don't stop being infantile after we're infants.
ko: 우리는 다 유아기부터 끝까지 유치해요. 유아 이후에 유치함이 끝나는 게 아니에요.

en: It's one thing to know it, you know, theoretically. And it's another thing to know it, know it.
ko: 이론적으로 아는 것과 정말 아는 것은 다른 거예요.

en: Like this thing ends. This ride ends. But that's the pain. It's the fact that the whole thing ends.
ko: 이게 끝난다는 것. 이 여행이 끝난다는 것. 하지만 그게 고통이에요. 전부 끝난다는 사실.

en: And when people fall off the edge, they fall off the edge. So yeah, the knowledge that it ends is the painful thing, not the actual moment of the  Yeah.
ko: 사람들이 가장자리에서 떨어지면 떨어지는 거예요. 끝난다는 지식이 고통스러운 거지, 실제 순간이 아니라.

en: Many times what makes moments really precious is that they're going to be gone. I think that's not a trivial thing for us humans to really contend with.
ko: 순간을 정말 소중하게 만드는 건 그것들이 사라진다는 거예요. 우리 인간이 진정으로 마주하기에 사소하지 않은 거라고 봐요.

en: I think religion, religious thought, the divine, I think help with that.
ko: 종교, 종교적 사고, 신성한 것이 도움이 된다고 봐요.

en: I think the big mistake for mankind was the creation of monotheistic religions because they brought evil into the world.
ko: 인류의 큰 실수는 유일신 종교의 창조였다고 봐요. 세상에 악을 가져왔으니까.

en: Because if you believe in a monotheistic religion, it means I'm right and you're wrong.
ko: 유일신 종교를 믿으면 내가 맞고 네가 틀리다는 의미가 되니까.

en: If you don't. So it's already on that footing. It's very dogmatic. Yeah.
ko: 이미 그런 토대 위에 서는 거예요. 매우 독단적이죠.

en: Dogmatic. And it's led to everything, inquisitions and this, you know, this kind of stuff.
ko: 독단적. 종교재판이나 그런 것들을 다 일으켰어요.

en: It's all as a result of it that one religion is superior and the other should be stamped out and all that.
ko: 한 종교가 우월하고 다른 건 박멸해야 한다는 것의 결과죠.

en: And in my opinion, monotheistic religion has generated the most fantastic amount of non-religious feeling.
ko: 제 생각에 유일신 종교는 가장 엄청난 양의 비종교적 감정을 만들어냈어요.

en: Whereas when you have all the different gods and have different specialities and the ones you like and the ones everybody likes and they have their temples and their offerings.
ko: 여러 신들이 있고 각각 전문 분야가 있고 좋아하는 신이 있고 신전과 제물이 있을 때는.

en: It was very interesting to me to go into a temple in Calcutta when I went there with my wife Joanna.
ko: 제 아내 조안나와 캘커타의 사원에 가봤는데 정말 흥미로웠어요.

en: And we went into the temples and saw how they were. And I think they must be very much like the ones in Mesopotamia.
ko: 사원에 들어가서 어떤 모습인지 봤는데 메소포타미아의 것들과 많이 비슷했을 거라고 봐요.

en: So there was never anything about them which affronted people's individuality or, I mean, there's no religious prejudice or even racial prejudice in antiquity.
ko: 사람들의 개인성을 거스르는 게 전혀 없었어요. 고대에는 종교적 편견이나 인종적 편견도 없었어요.

en: All these things are modern, disadvantageous matters. If you think what's done in the name of religion, it is absolutely staggering.
ko: 이런 것들은 다 현대의 불리한 문제들이에요. 종교의 이름으로 행해진 걸 생각하면 정말 놀랍습니다.
